does: This paper introduces CAIA, a benchmark dataset of 178 time-anchored financial decision-making tasks that evaluates how well AI agents resist adversarial manipulation in high-stakes cryptocurrency and financial markets. The authors test 17 state-of-the-art language models on their ability to distinguish accurate information from deliberate misinformation, navigate fragmented and contradictory data sources, and make irreversible financial decisions under adversarial pressure. The core finding is a dramatic capability gap: unaided frontier models achieve only 28% accuracy on tasks that junior analysts routinely solve, and even with tool access, models plateau at 67.4% accuracy versus an 80% human baseline. The paper identifies a critical failure mode where models systematically choose unreliable information sources, preferring generic web search over authoritative financial databases despite having access to both.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research examining failure modes in large language models deployed in real-world applications, extending prior work on hallucination and factual inconsistency from controlled benchmarks to adversarial settings where errors have measurable financial consequences. It responds to limitations in existing AI evaluation frameworks that measure task completion in benign environments but fail to test robustness against deception or systematic pressure. The paper connects to research on agent architecture and tool use, demonstrating that simply augmenting models with access to external information does not solve the underlying problem of poor source discrimination. It also engages with studies on adversarial robustness and agent alignment, showing that even frontier models struggle with adversarial scenarios that humans navigate competently.
enables: This benchmark and its findings enable future research into building more robust AI agents for high-stakes domains by establishing cryptocurrency markets as a naturalistic testbed where errors have quantifiable costs and adversarial pressure is real. The systematic identification of tool selection failures points toward new approaches for improving model judgment in source reliability and decision confidence calibration. This work supports development of better evaluation methodologies that stress-test agents under conditions closer to real-world deployment, including adversarial information environments. It may inspire research into architectural improvements for agent decision-making, such as explicit uncertainty quantification, adversarial training, or novel approaches to source evaluation. More broadly, the benchmark and findings establish that capability measurement requires not just task completion metrics but also resilience assessment, opening avenues for safer deployment frameworks in financial and other high-stakes domains.
